reason: Both answers correctly identify the same core actions: push back but don't walk away, cap liability around 12 months of fees (with a 2x fallback), narrow (rather than delete) the IP indemnity with standard carve-outs and a remedy ladder, add a mutual consequential-damages waiver, and frame the ask as standard for a small vendor. Both correctly note the asymmetry/existential-risk argument for unlimited liability and recommend a quick lawyer redline plus getting E&O/cyber insurance. Where they diverge, B includes several additional, genuinely useful items A omits: checking auto-renewal terms, payment terms (net 30 vs. 60/90), making sure the customer isn't claiming ownership of the underlying platform (vs. just their data/configs), and reconciling insurance requirements the startup may not be able to meet. These are concrete, decision-relevant additions, not restatements. A's unique points (don't indemnify the customer's downstream customers, an explicit walk-away trigger) are reasonable but narrower and largely reinforce shared points. B's texting-style shorthand is a minor tone issue but doesn't undermine correctness or usefulness. Overall B is marginally more complete while remaining equally actionable.
